title: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality

items.subtopic: Diagnosis: Reducing Errors and Improving Quality
items.title: High Misdiagnosis Rates in Common Internal Medicine Conditions
items.content: Misdiagnoses are prevalent in common internal medicine conditions, with rates of mislabeling or missed diagnoses ranging from 20% to 50%. This highlights the critical need for improved diagnostic accuracy and awareness.
keyPoints: Common diseases like asthma, pulmonary embolism, congestive heart failure, seizures, strokes, ruptured aneurysms, depression, and cancer are frequently misdiagnosed.
Misdiagnosis rates range from 20% to 50%, encompassing both false positives and missed or delayed diagnoses.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · Diagnosis: Reducing Errors and Improving Quality

items.subtopic: Diagnosis: Reducing Errors and Improving Quality
items.title: Multidisciplinary Approach to Enhancing Diagnostic Accuracy
items.content: Improvements in diagnostic accuracy are not solely reliant on advanced medical technologies but also involve integrating diverse scientific disciplines. This multidisciplinary approach enhances the reliability of diagnoses.
keyPoints: Diagnostic improvements can arise from fields such as systems engineering, cognitive psychology, clinical epidemiology, and health information technology.
Artificial intelligence and clinical decision support are key components in advancing diagnostic accuracy.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · Diagnosis: Reducing Errors and Improving Quality

items.subtopic: EMERGENCE OF DIAGNOSIS ERROR AS AN IMPORTANT PATIENT SAFETY ISSUE | NEW WAYS TO THINK ABOUT DIAGNOSIS AND DIAGNOSTIC ERRORS
items.title: Understanding the Impact of Diagnostic Errors on Patient Safety
items.content: Diagnostic errors are a significant patient safety issue, being the leading type of medical error. They occur when there is a failure to accurately and timely establish or communicate an explanation for a patient's health problem. The intersection of various failures in the diagnostic process can lead to severe outcomes, including death.
keyPoints: Diagnostic errors are defined as the failure to establish or communicate an accurate diagnosis.
Conservative estimates suggest that 40,000–80,000 patients die annually in U.S. hospitals due to diagnostic errors.
Errors can occur at multiple points in the diagnostic process without necessarily resulting in harm.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · EMERGENCE OF DIAGNOSIS ERROR AS AN IMPORTANT PATIENT SAFETY ISSUE | NEW WAYS TO THINK ABOUT DIAGNOSIS AND DIAGNOSTIC ERRORS

items.subtopic: EMERGENCE OF DIAGNOSIS ERROR AS AN IMPORTANT PATIENT SAFETY ISSUE | NEW WAYS TO THINK ABOUT DIAGNOSIS AND DIAGNOSTIC ERRORS
items.title: Cognitive Biases and Errors in Clinical Reasoning
items.content: Clinical reasoning errors, categorized into hasty judgments, biased judgments, and inaccurate probability estimates, are a major source of diagnostic errors. Cognitive psychology research identifies numerous heuristics that can lead to efficient diagnosis but also introduce biases.
keyPoints: Errors in clinical reasoning fall into three categories: hasty judgments, biased judgments, and inaccurate probability estimates.
Cognitive heuristics can facilitate or hinder accurate diagnosis depending on their application.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · EMERGENCE OF DIAGNOSIS ERROR AS AN IMPORTANT PATIENT SAFETY ISSUE | NEW WAYS TO THINK ABOUT DIAGNOSIS AND DIAGNOSTIC ERRORS

items.subtopic: TABLE 10-1 National Academy of Medicine Recommendations for Improving Diagnosis in Health Care
items.title: Understanding Cognitive Biases in Diagnostic Errors
items.content: Cognitive biases significantly impact diagnostic accuracy by affecting how clinicians process information and make decisions. Recognizing these biases is crucial to improving diagnosis quality.
keyPoints: Premature closure: Accepting a diagnosis before full verification can lead to errors.
Anchoring: Fixating on initial symptoms or data without adjusting leads to misdiagnosis.
Confirmation bias: Seeking evidence that supports rather than refutes hypotheses skews diagnostic accuracy.
Search satisfying: Stopping the search for additional diagnoses after finding an initial explanation is risky.
Availability bias: Overweighting recent or dramatic cases can distort clinical judgment.
Base-rate neglect: Ignoring disease prevalence, especially with tests having a 5% false-positive rate in low-prevalence populations, leads to misinterpretation of results.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · TABLE 10-1 National Academy of Medicine Recommendations for Improving Diagnosis in Health Care

items.subtopic: TABLE 10-1 National Academy of Medicine Recommendations for Improving Diagnosis in Health Care
items.title: Systemic Approach to Overcoming Diagnostic Limitations
items.content: A systemic understanding of diagnosis is essential, as it addresses not only cognitive biases but also other factors like memory limitations and system process failures.
keyPoints: Diagnosis should be viewed as a 'system' rather than just mental processes.
Limitations include human memory constraints, information shortfalls, and constrained encounter time.
Many patient symptoms are self-limited and do not indicate harmful outcomes, challenging traditional diagnostic approaches.
Safety and cognitive sciences suggest rethinking diagnosis to overcome these limitations.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · TABLE 10-1 National Academy of Medicine Recommendations for Improving Diagnosis in Health Care

items.subtopic: UNCERTAINTY IN DIAGNOSIS | DON'T MISS DIAGNOSES AND RED FLAGS
items.title: Embrace Diagnostic Uncertainty with Safety Nets
items.content: In primary care, definitive diagnoses are often impractical early in an illness due to variations in patient presentation and test performance. Clinicians should use uncertainty to enhance situational awareness and create safety nets against delayed or misdiagnosis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis is often imprecise; terms like preliminary diagnosis and differential diagnosis should be used.
Clinicians must communicate uncertainties while respecting patients' concerns, especially for anxious patients worried about serious conditions.
Tailor communication to patient's health literacy and trust levels.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · UNCERTAINTY IN DIAGNOSIS | DON'T MISS DIAGNOSES AND RED FLAGS

items.subtopic: UNCERTAINTY IN DIAGNOSIS | DON'T MISS DIAGNOSES AND RED FLAGS
items.title: Identify Critical 'Don't Miss' Diagnoses
items.content: Despite uncertainty, clinicians must remain vigilant for diseases that progress rapidly or require specific treatments. Systems should be designed to consider critical diagnoses, especially those with public health implications.
keyPoints: Be aware of common and less common 'don’t miss' diagnoses (e.g., acute myocardial infarction, sepsis).
Recognize presentations where critical diagnoses may lurk.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · UNCERTAINTY IN DIAGNOSIS | DON'T MISS DIAGNOSES AND RED FLAGS

items.subtopic: UNCERTAINTY IN DIAGNOSIS | DON'T MISS DIAGNOSES AND RED FLAGS
items.title: Utilize Red Flags to Identify Serious Pathologies
items.content: Red flags are clinical clues that suggest more serious underlying conditions. They help reassure clinicians and patients about the urgency of symptoms like back pain or headache.
keyPoints: Examples of red flags for back pain include fever, weight loss, history of malignancy, intravenous drug use, or neurologic signs.
Red flag clues should be used to consider more serious etiologies in various presenting syndromes.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · UNCERTAINTY IN DIAGNOSIS | DON'T MISS DIAGNOSES AND RED FLAGS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC PITFALLS | DIAGNOSIS SAFETY CULTURE
items.title: Avoiding Diagnostic Pitfalls in Breast Cancer
items.content: In breast cancer diagnosis, relying solely on a normal mammogram to rule out cancer when a palpable lump is present can lead to missed diagnoses. Further assessment and biopsy are necessary for unexplained masses.
keyPoints: A palpable breast lump requires further testing beyond a normal mammogram.
Diagnostic pitfalls include misinterpreting negative test results as definitive in the presence of physical findings.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · DIAGNOSTIC PITFALLS | DIAGNOSIS SAFETY CULTURE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC PITFALLS | DIAGNOSIS SAFETY CULTURE
items.title: Importance of a Safety Culture in Diagnosis
items.content: A safety culture that encourages openness and learning is crucial for accurate diagnosis. Patients should feel empowered to question diagnoses, especially if symptoms persist or change unexpectedly.
keyPoints: Safety culture involves open communication about errors without fear of retribution.
Encouragement for staff and patients to discuss problems leads to better diagnostic outcomes.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · DIAGNOSTIC PITFALLS | DIAGNOSIS SAFETY CULTURE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSTIC PITFALLS | DIAGNOSIS SAFETY CULTURE
items.title: Attributes of a Reliable Diagnostic Safety Culture
items.content: A reliable safety culture in diagnosis includes communication about errors, openness to discussing problems, learning from mistakes, leadership commitment to safety, and accountability for safety events.
keyPoints: Safety measurement tools assess staff willingness to report mistakes.
Leadership should prioritize safety over production speed.
Accountability is crucial for following up on safety concerns.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · DIAGNOSTIC PITFALLS | DIAGNOSIS SAFETY CULTURE

items.subtopic: HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND THE FUTURE OF DIAGNOSIS | DIAGNOSIS OF DIAGNOSIS ERRORS AND SAFETY: PRACTICAL CONCLUSIONS
items.title: Harnessing Health Information Technology for Improved Diagnosis
items.content: Health information technology (HIT) is increasingly central to diagnostic processes, with clinicians spending more time interacting with computers than patients. While HIT has been criticized for distracting from patient interaction and being designed around non-diagnostic needs like billing, there's potential to redesign these systems to better support diagnosis by leveraging AI capabilities.
keyPoints: Clinicians spend more time on computers than with patients for diagnostic tasks.
Electronic medical records need radical redesign to focus on supporting diagnostics rather than administrative tasks.
AI has significant potential in image and data analysis, pattern recognition, and decision support but faces challenges in accuracy and incorporating human elements.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND THE FUTURE OF DIAGNOSIS | DIAGNOSIS OF DIAGNOSIS ERRORS AND SAFETY: PRACTICAL CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND THE FUTURE OF DIAGNOSIS | DIAGNOSIS OF DIAGNOSIS ERRORS AND SAFETY: PRACTICAL CONCLUSIONS
items.title: Critical Questions for Improving Diagnostic Reliability and Timeliness
items.content: To enhance diagnostic reliability and timeliness, clinicians should regularly ask themselves key questions about their patients. These include considering differential diagnoses, identifying unexplained abnormalities, and recognizing critical diagnoses to avoid missing. Additionally, systemic questions about tracking systems, safety culture, and the role of medical records in diagnosis are crucial for minimizing errors.
keyPoints: Ask 'What else might this be?' to ensure a comprehensive differential diagnosis.
'What doesn't fit?' helps identify unexplained abnormalities that shouldn't be dismissed.
Consider 'What critical diagnoses are important not to miss?' to avoid overlooking red flags and pitfalls.
Evaluate the effectiveness of closed-loop systems for tracking patient symptoms and findings.
Assess the culture-of-safety within the organization and how medical records impact diagnosis.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND THE FUTURE OF DIAGNOSIS | DIAGNOSIS OF DIAGNOSIS ERRORS AND SAFETY: PRACTICAL CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND THE FUTURE OF DIAGNOSIS | DIAGNOSIS OF DIAGNOSIS ERRORS AND SAFETY: PRACTICAL CONCLUSIONS
items.title: Transforming Diagnostic Safety Culture for Quality Improvement
items.content: Improving diagnostic safety culture involves recognizing and learning from errors, ensuring reliable follow-up and feedback, leveraging health IT effectively, and partnering with patients. Efforts following the National Academy of Medicine report have aimed at understanding and preventing diagnostic errors through quality improvement and research.
keyPoints: Diagnostic errors can be reduced by transforming safety culture and learning from mistakes.
Reliable tracking, follow-up, and feedback systems are essential for minimizing diagnostic errors.
Health IT should be leveraged to support diagnosis, requiring a shift in its design focus.
Partnership with patients is crucial for enhancing the quality of diagnosis.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · HEALTH INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY AND THE FUTURE OF DIAGNOSIS | DIAGNOSIS OF DIAGNOSIS ERRORS AND SAFETY: PRACTICAL CONCLUSIONS

"An x-ray not followed up by a physician."
items.explanation: The NAM defines diagnostic error as failing to establish or communicate an accurate and timely explanation of the patient's health problem(s).

items.subtopic: TABLE 10-1 National Academy of Medicine Recommendations for Improving Diagnosis in Health Care
items.question: What does 'search satisfying' refer to in the context of diagnostic errors?
options: The tendency to prematurely accept a diagnosis without full verification.
The tendency to call off a search once an initial piece of data or explanation is found, neglecting further investigation.
The inclination to focus on recent dramatic cases when diagnosing.
The failure to consider the prevalence of a disease in interpreting test results.
items.explanation: 'Search satisfying' refers to stopping the diagnostic search once initial data or an explanation is found, without further investigation.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · TABLE 10-1 National Academy of Medicine Recommendations for Improving Diagnosis in Health Care

items.question: Which bias involves giving too much weight to diagnoses that are more readily recalled?
items.explanation: 'Availability bias' is the tendency to give undue weight to diagnoses that come more easily to mind.

items.question: What is a common diagnostic pitfall in breast cancer diagnosis?
options: Ordering an ultrasound for all patients with breast lumps.
Reassuring a patient that cancer is ruled out by a normal mammogram when there's a palpable lump.
Performing a biopsy on every palpable mass without further testing.
Ignoring patient-reported symptoms if the mammogram is normal.
items.explanation: A common pitfall is reassuring a patient that cancer is ruled out by a normal mammogram when there's a palpable lump, which requires further assessment.

items.question: What should be done for an unexplained mass or lesion that is palpable on physical examination?

Disregard it if no symptoms are present.
Assess further including additional testing and biopsy where warranted.
Only monitor without any immediate action.
items.explanation: An unexplained mass or lesion palpable on physical examination should be assessed further, including additional testing and biopsy if necessary.

items.assertion: Hindsight bias should be applied when judging missed diagnoses.
items.reason: Clinicians often face challenges in making diagnoses, and caution is needed.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because hindsight bias should not be applied; the reason is true as clinicians do face diagnostic challenges.
items.reference: Diagnosis Reducing Errors and Improving Quality · DIAGNOSTIC PITFALLS | DIAGNOSIS SAFETY CULTURE

items.assertion: Generative AI is expected to take over the job of making diagnoses.
items.reason: AI has significant capabilities for image and data analysis, pattern recognition, creating clinical notes, and decision support.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not fully explain why AI is expected to take over making diagnoses due to fundamental limitations mentioned.

items.assertion: Clinicians should ask themselves questions about the systems they work in to improve diagnosis.
items.reason: This helps ensure reliability, timeliness, and minimizes potential for errors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clinicians should ask these questions.
